threats.title: setRouter() Inverted Zero-Address Check Permanently Bricks Router Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.setRouter() has require(_router == address(0)) instead of require(_router != address(0)). This means the function only accepts address(0) as valid input. If called, it sets router to zero, causing all future launch() calls to revert. No valid router address can ever be set via this function.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code is demonstrably wrong. The require condition is inverted—it should reject zero addresses but instead requires them. If the owner calls setRouter(address(0)), the router becomes zero and all genesis pool launches will revert when calling IRouter(address(0)).addLiquidity(). The owner cannot fix this since setRouter only accepts address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_router == address(0), 'ZA') should be != instead of ==

preconditions: Owner attempts to update router address via setRouter()
Current router address was set during initialize()

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Only address(0) passes the check, setting router to zero bricks all launches
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Correct check should be require(_router != address(0))

analysis_questions: Is the router currently set to a valid address from initialize()?
Are there other mechanisms to update the router besides setRouter()?
If setRouter(address(0)) is called, can any genesis pool ever launch?
validation_requirements: Verify that calling setRouter with a valid address reverts
Verify that calling setRouter(address(0)) succeeds and bricks launches
Check if there's an alternative path to update the router

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If owner ever calls setRouter(address(0)) (perhaps trying to test or misunderstanding the code), all genesis pool launches permanently fail. There is no way to set a valid router address through this function.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All genesis pool launches fail permanently; native tokens and funding deposits locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis pool participants and token owners
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only via contract upgrade (GenesisPoolManager is upgradeable)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for setRouter with valid address

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Would be caught on first attempted router update

threats.title: Thenian mintPublic Reentrancy via _safeMint Bypasses Per-Wallet NFT Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: Thenian.mintPublic() uses _safeMint which triggers onERC721Received callback on contract recipients. The per-wallet limit check (balanceOf + amount <= 15) occurs only at function entry. During _safeMint's callback, an attacker contract can re-enter mintPublic with a new amount that passes the stale balanceOf check, then the outer loop continues minting beyond the 15-token limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: mintPublic checks balanceOf(msg.sender).add(amount) <= 15 at entry, then enters a loop calling _safeMint. After the first _safeMint, balanceOf is 1, but the outer loop expects to mint 9 more. If the attacker re-enters with amount=10 during the callback (check: 1+10=11 <= 15 passes), the inner call mints 10 tokens (balanceOf becomes 11), then the outer loop continues minting 9 more, reaching balanceOf=20 > 15.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf check at entry, _safeMint in loop enables reentrancy; no nonReentrant guard

preconditions: Public sale period is active (timestamp within 2-5 days after SALE_START_TIMESTAMP)
Attacker deploys a contract that implements onERC721Received to re-enter mintPublic
totalSupply() + total minted < MAX_SUPPLY
Attacker provides sufficient ETH/AVAX for all NFT payments

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-wallet limit checked once at entry; _safeMint callback allows re-entry before loop completes
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After re-entry mints additional tokens, outer loop continues minting, total exceeds 15 per wallet
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier on mintPublic; no per-iteration balance check

analysis_questions: Does _safeMint update balanceOf before the callback (yes, standard OZ behavior)?
How many total NFTs can be minted via nested reentrancy?
Does this impact mintFirst or mintSecond as well?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract that re-enters mintPublic in onERC721Received
Verify final balanceOf exceeds 15 for the attacker contract
Calculate maximum mintable via deep reentrancy chains

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker contract calls mintPublic(10) with 10*NFT_PRICE. During first _safeMint callback, re-enters mintPublic(10) with 10*NFT_PRICE. Inner check: balanceOf(1)+10=11<=15 passes. Inner mints 10. Outer resumes minting 9 more. Final: 20 NFTs for one wallet, bypassing 15-per-wallet limit. Deeper reentrancy can push this further.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker mints more NFTs than the 15-per-wallet limit allows, violating scarcity guarantees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All NFT holders (diluted relative scarcity per intended distribution)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for mintPublic via contract callers

threats.title: Governor.sol Quorum Uses Current Total Supply Instead of Snapshot Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction, the quorum() function calls token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without passing the blockTimestamp parameter, meaning quorum is calculated against the current total supply rather than the supply at the proposal's snapshot time. This allows quorum manipulation after proposal creation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function signature accepts blockTimestamp but ignores it: `return (token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator()`. Compare with L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction.sol which correctly uses: `return (token.getPastTotalSupply(blockTimestamp) * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator()`. This means quorum threshold changes with current supply, not snapshot supply.

proof_of_signal.pattern: token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() called without blockTimestamp parameter; ignores function argument

preconditions: Governor.sol variant is deployed
smNFT total supply changes between proposal creation and state evaluation
Attacker or external events can influence smNFT total supply

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without timestamp vs L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction.sol's getPastTotalSupply(blockTimestamp)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Quorum threshold is dynamic post-proposal, allowing retroactive manipulation

analysis_questions: Which Governor variant is deployed?
Can smNFT total supply be influenced by any actor?
What is the practical magnitude of supply changes between proposal and execution?
validation_requirements: Verify quorum returns different values at different total supply levels for the same proposalId
Test: create proposal, change supply, verify quorum changes retroactively
Assess whether supply changes are frequent enough to exploit

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a malicious proposal when smNFT supply is low (easy quorum). A few supporters vote for it. Before execution, total supply is checked at current value. If supply decreased further, quorum is even easier. If supply increased, attacker could strategically time the execution window.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Governance proposals pass or fail based on manipulated quorum thresholds

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying quorum uses snapshot supply

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient smNFTs to influence supply or meet quorum
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block between proposal creation and voting deadline
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Quorum changes visible via quorum() queries

threats.title: Incentives Mapping Not Zeroed After transferIncentives Causes Irrecoverable Funds Display
threats.hypothesis_description: After transferIncentives() sends incentive tokens to the external bribe, the incentives mapping retains non-zero values. If the pool later transitions to NOT_QUALIFIED (via setPoolStatus), claimIncentives() will attempt to transfer tokens the pool no longer holds, reverting via SafeERC20 and permanently preventing any incentive claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: transferIncentives() approves and sends tokens to external_bribe but never sets incentives[token] = 0. The _setPoolStatus function allows transitioning to NOT_QUALIFIED from any state when called by manager. In NOT_QUALIFIED state, claimIncentives() iterates incentiveTokens and tries to transfer incentives[token] amounts, but the balance is zero, causing permanent revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: transferIncentives sends tokens via notifyRewardAmount but never zeros incentives[token]

preconditions: Genesis pool has incentives added
Pool reaches PRE_LAUNCH state (transferIncentives called)
Manager calls setPoolStatus(NOT_QUALIFIED) after incentive transfer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: incentives[token] never zeroed in transferIncentives; claimIncentives reads stale non-zero values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Token owner sees claimable incentives but transfer reverts due to zero balance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: transferIncentives should set incentives[incentiveTokens[i]] = 0 after sending

analysis_questions: Can manager set pool to NOT_QUALIFIED after PRE_LAUNCH?
Is there any path to recover incentive tokens from external bribe?
Does claimableIncentives mislead the tokenOwner?
validation_requirements: Trace: addIncentives -> transferIncentives -> setPoolStatus(NOT_QUALIFIED) -> claimIncentives
Verify claimIncentives reverts when balance is zero
Check if external bribe has recovery mechanism

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Token owner adds incentives. Pool progresses to PRE_LAUNCH, incentives sent to bribe. Manager sets NOT_QUALIFIED (e.g., external issue). Token owner calls claimIncentives, transaction reverts because pool has no incentive token balance. Incentive tokens are unrecoverable.
prerequisites: Pool transitions back to NOT_QUALIFIED after incentive transfer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Token owner permanently loses all incentive tokens deposited

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for claim after transfer + status change

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N/A — affects token owner's deposited incentives
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Token owner discovers when trying to claim

threats.title: Gauge emissions diversion via unvalidated genesisPool address in approveGenesisPool() (pair address confusion)
threats.hypothesis_description: Governance can approve a nativeToken, but the manager later launches using genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) (which may change) rather than the specific pool that was approved and stored in liveNativeTokens. If a new GenesisPool is created for the same nativeToken after approval (or factory mapping is altered by genesisManager), epoch flip may launch a different pool than the one approved, potentially staking liquidity into a gauge tied to an unintended pair and diverting emissions/launch liquidity flows.
threats.investigation_rationale: liveNativeTokens tracks nativeToken only; it does not bind to a specific pool address. checkAtEpochFlip resolves pool address dynamically per nativeToken each time. If the factory can return a different pool for same nativeToken over time, the system can launch/attach gauges for a pool that was never approved/verified (pair-empty checks, etc.), breaking lifecycle assumptions and enabling emissions misdirection.
deployment_assumptions: genesisFactory.genesisManager can create multiple pools for the same nativeToken over time
The currently returned genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) can differ from the pool that was approved earlier
Attacker can influence pool creation timing via privileged genesisManager compromise or misconfiguration
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script evidence showing genesisFactory ownership/manager immutability; GenesisPoolFactory.setGenesisManager exists and can change manager.

proof_of_signal.pattern: liveNativeTokens stores only nativeToken; later checkAtEpochFlip resolves genesisPool via genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) at runtime

preconditions: A second GenesisPool instance for the same nativeToken can exist in GenesisPoolFactory.genesisPools[nativeToken]
genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) returns the last non-NOT_QUALIFIED pool (dynamic pointer)
liveNativeTokens contains the nativeToken from an earlier approval, but factory pointer has since changed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: token-keyed tracking without binding to a specific pool address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: launching a different pool than approved breaks pair emptiness checks and can mis-route gauge creation / liquidity deposit
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mapping nativeToken=>approvedGenesisPoolAddress; no re-validation of pool identity at launch

analysis_questions: Can a new GenesisPool be created for the same nativeToken after approval without removing nativeToken from liveNativeTokens?
Can GenesisPoolFactory.setGenesisManager be used to change manager and create new pools mid-flight?
Does checkAtEpochFlip ever launch a pool whose pairAddress differs from the pair created/approved earlier?
validation_requirements: Simulate approveGenesisPool(nativeToken) then create a new pool for same nativeToken and verify checkAtEpochFlip launches the new pool
Confirm factory/manager permissions and whether poolStatus transitions can force getGenesisPool to point elsewhere

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If governance approves a token and adds it to liveNativeTokens, then later a different genesisPool becomes the factory's current pool for that nativeToken, epoch flip will operate on the wrong pool: it may create/attach gauge and stake liquidity for an unintended pair, potentially diverting emissions and breaking the intended launch escrow.
prerequisites: Ability to cause a new pool to be created for same nativeToken or modify factory manager (privileged or misconfigured path)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential diversion of gauge emissions and mis-launch of liquidity, leading to significant misallocation or loss if liquidity is added/staked for a wrong pair
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Depositors and tokenOwner for the affected nativeToken; potentially protocol-wide gauge emissions routing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult; would require governance intervention and possibly redeploying gauges/pools

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing lifecycle tests that bind a specific pool address to a nativeToken across approval->launch

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block; state change between approval and epoch flip
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain events: ApprovedGenesisPool vs actual launched pool address and gauge.setGenesisPool target

threats.title: Unsafe ERC20 approve pattern can permanently DoS incentive transfer/launch due to non-zero allowance requirement
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.transferIncentives() and _approveTokens() use SafeERC20.safeApprove(router/external_bribe, amount) without first setting allowance to 0. Many ERC20s (e.g., USDT-style) require allowance to be set to 0 before changing it; safeApprove will revert if allowance is non-zero. This can permanently block transferring incentives to bribes or adding liquidity during launch if approvals were previously set.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code repeatedly calls safeApprove in loops and across lifecycle transitions. If called twice with a non-zero existing allowance, it can brick PRE_LAUNCH transition and launch. This is a common real-world failure mode.
deployment_assumptions: At least one incentive token or native/funding token enforces 'approve from non-zero to non-zero' revert semantics
transferIncentives() or launch() can be called more than once for the same spender (router/bribe) with a non-zero existing allowance (e.g., retry after partial failure)
threats.config_evidence: No token allowlist constraints shown that exclude USDT-like approve behavior; SafeERC20.safeApprove used directly in GenesisPool.sol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeApprove(router/external_bribe, amount) without allowance reset to 0

preconditions: Existing allowance(router/external_bribe) for the token is non-zero
Function is called again with a non-zero target allowance
Token reverts on changing non-zero allowance to non-zero

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: PRE_LAUNCH and LAUNCH can become uncallable (DoS) due to approval reverts
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No safeIncreaseAllowance or approve(0) then approve(amount)

analysis_questions: Can transferIncentives() be called more than once (e.g., manager retries after partial loop failure)?
Can launch() be retried after a revert leaving allowances set?
Are connector tokens known to include USDT-like tokens on Avalanche?
validation_requirements: Unit test with mock token enforcing approve(0) requirement
Simulate calling transferIncentives twice and observe revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Even without a malicious attacker, a failed attempt followed by retry can brick the pool. A malicious token owner could intentionally include a token with strict approve semantics as an incentive token to force a DoS at pre-launch, blocking pool progression and locking deposits until governance intervention.
prerequisites: Token selection includes strict-approve tokens or incentives include such tokens

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: prevents incentives distribution and/or liquidity launch, potentially locking user deposits and native token escrow
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors and token owner in affected genesis pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract changes or manual token handling; likely unrecoverable without privileged upgrades/migrations

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for USDT-style approve semantics on incentive/funding/native tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain revert reason patterns around approve

threats.title: AuctionFactory.replaceAuction/removeAuction can brick genesis pools via zero auction selection and lack of validation in setGenesisPoolInfo
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken selects auction from AuctionFactory by index and falls back to auctions(0) if the index slot is empty. If auctions(0) is removed (set to address(0)) or replaced incorrectly, depositNativeToken can pass address(0) to GenesisPool.setGenesisPoolInfo without validation. Later, GenesisPool.depositToken will revert when calling auction.getNativeTokenAmount/getFundingTokenAmount on address(0), bricking deposits and potentially trapping native token escrow.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic configuration/ops hazard: AuctionFactory allows setting entries to address(0), and there is no require(_auction != 0) in setGenesisPoolInfo. Even without malicious intent, a mistaken removal can break new genesis pools.
deployment_assumptions: AuctionFactory.auctions[0] can be removed or set to address(0) in production
GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken may be called while auctions(0) is zero
No external guard prevents using address(0) auction
threats.config_evidence: AuctionFactory.removeAuction(_pos) sets auctions[_pos]=address(0) and does not prevent _pos==0; GenesisPool.setGenesisPoolInfo has no non-zero check for _auction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeAuction sets auctions[_pos]=address(0); replaceAuction does not bound-check _pos; manager selection falls back to auctions(0) without checking non-zero

preconditions: AuctionFactory.auctions(0) returns address(0) OR depositNativeToken selects an index that is address(0) and fallback is also address(0)
GenesisPool is initialized with auction=0
Any subsequent depositToken call reaches _getNativeTokenAmount/_getFundingTokenAmount

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unvalidated external dependency address stored in state
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bricks deposits and potentially lifecycle progression for affected pools
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: require(auction != address(0)) in manager and/or setGenesisPoolInfo

analysis_questions: Are there deployment scripts ensuring auctions[0] is immutable or never removed?
Does governance have a process preventing removal of default auction?
What happens to already-created pools if auction is later changed via setAuction?
validation_requirements: Simulate AuctionFactory.removeAuction(0) then depositNativeToken and attempt depositToken
Add a unit test asserting setGenesisPoolInfo rejects address(0) auction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Operator removes default auction (pos 0) as part of maintenance. New genesis pools created afterward become unusable because deposit conversions revert, potentially locking tokenOwner’s deposited native tokens in the pool until rejection/claimNative paths are used.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: locks functionality and may lock escrowed native tokens temporarily; not a direct drain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Token owners creating genesis pools and depositors trying to participate
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium: governance can setAuction (if still in NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED) or reject pool

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for AuctionFactory index 0 removal and null-auction initialization

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor pools with auction==0 and deposit reverts

threats.title: Thenian.reserveNFTs() reentrancy via ERC721Receiver can exceed MAX_RESERVE or distort reservedAmount accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: Thenian.reserveNFTs() mints via _safeMint in a loop and updates reservedAmount only after minting completes. If _to is a contract implementing onERC721Received, it can re-enter reserveNFTs (owner-only reduces attacker access) or other minting functions during the callback. Because reservedAmount is not incremented until after the loop, reentrancy can bypass the MAX_RESERVE check within the same transaction and cause reservedAmount to undercount relative to minted reserves.
threats.investigation_rationale: Although onlyOwner restricts direct exploitation, real deployments often use multisigs/contracts as owner or as _to address. If owner is a contract with complex callback behavior (or compromised), this can cause invariant breaks and over-minting of reserved NFTs.
deployment_assumptions: Owner or _to is a contract capable of reentering (via onERC721Received) and owner privileges are exercised through that contract
Owner calls reserveNFTs with _to set to a contract address (common for treasuries)
Reentrant call path exists to reserveNFTs again before reservedAmount is updated
threats.config_evidence: Thenian.sol uses _safeMint in reserveNFTs and updates reservedAmount after loop; no nonReentrant guard. Owner/multiSig are set; multiSig is an EOA hardcoded but owner may be a contract in deployment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeMint inside loop; reservedAmount updated after loop; no reentrancy guard

preconditions: Caller is owner()
_to is a contract implementing onERC721Received
onERC721Received re-enters reserveNFTs (or another minting path) before reservedAmount is updated

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: state updated after external callback-capable minting
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: MAX_RESERVE check can be bypassed intra-tx; reservedAmount may not reflect actual reserved mints
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: nonReentrant or updating reservedAmount before mint loop

analysis_questions: Can owner be a contract wallet in production (e.g., Gnosis Safe)?
Are there any other functions callable during callback that influence supply controls?
Does ERC721Enumerable update totalSupply in a way that is consistent under reentrancy?
validation_requirements: Create a malicious receiver contract and simulate owner calling reserveNFTs to itself with reentrancy
Check whether reservedAmount and totalSupply exceed expected MAX_RESERVE behavior

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner reserves NFTs to a contract treasury. The treasury’s onERC721Received triggers another reserveNFTs call (or other mint paths). Because reservedAmount isn’t incremented yet, MAX_RESERVE can be exceeded, breaking reserve policy and potentially MAX_SUPPLY planning.
prerequisites: Contract-based owner or recipient with reentrant behavior

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: supply policy violation; potential dilution and loss of trust/value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: NFT holders and community relying on MAX_RESERVE guarantees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low once minted; requires social/governance remedy

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing reentrancy tests for reserveNFTs with contract recipients

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare reservedAmount vs number of reserved mints to treasury addresses

threats.title: Governance Bypass: Defeated and Expired Proposals Can Be Executed
threats.hypothesis_description: Both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() functions allow execution of proposals in Defeated, Expired, and Succeeded states. An attacker can create a malicious governance proposal containing arbitrary external calls, have it be voted down by the community, and still execute the malicious payload by calling execute().
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement explicitly allows Defeated and Expired states: `require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)`. This completely nullifies the governance voting mechanism and allows any proposer to execute arbitrary actions through the governor contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require allows ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired in addition to Succeeded

preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to meet proposalThreshold (default 0, so any holder)
Governor contract has been deployed and holds assets or controls privileged operations
Voting period completes (proposal reaches Defeated or Expired state)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: execute() requires status == Succeeded || Defeated || Expired instead of just Succeeded
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit proposal with arbitrary calldata targeting governor-controlled assets, let it be defeated, call execute()
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All assets controlled by governance are at risk - arbitrary contract calls are executed via _execute loop

economic_questions: What assets does the governor control or can relay to?
What is the proposalThreshold - can any token holder submit proposals?
Are there timelocks or additional guards on execution?
validation_requirements: Verify both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() have the same bug
Confirm state() function returns Defeated/Expired correctly
Test end-to-end: propose, defeat, execute malicious payload

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for propose() + execute() calls (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to total value controlled by governance
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI if governance controls significant assets

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after voting period ends

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users and governance participants
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets accessible via governor execute/relay functions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify state machine transitions for Defeated proposals

threats.title: Unvalidated Genesis Pool Address Allows Gauge Creation for Arbitrary Pairs
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() does not validate that the genesisPool address parameter was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract implementing IGenesisPool that returns true from depositToken(), triggering _preLaunchPool() which creates a real gauge for an attacker-controlled pair address and whitelists an arbitrary token.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function accepts any non-zero address as genesisPool. A malicious contract can return controlled values from getGenesisInfo() and getLiquidityPoolInfo(), causing gaugeManager.createGauge() to be called with an arbitrary pair address and tokenHandler.whitelistToken() with an arbitrary token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No factory validation on genesisPool parameter; any address accepted if non-zero

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a contract implementing IGenesisPool interface
gaugeManager.createGauge() does not independently validate the pair was created by pairFactory
No additional access control on depositToken beyond nonReentrant

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy FakeGenesisPool returning true from depositToken and attacker-controlled pairAddress from getLiquidityPoolInfo
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Redirect gauge emissions to attacker-controlled pair; whitelist arbitrary tokens for trading

economic_questions: Does gaugeManager.createGauge validate the pool address against the factory?
What emissions can be redirected to a fraudulently created gauge?
Can arbitrary token whitelisting be weaponized for price manipulation?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock FakeGenesisPool and call depositToken through manager
Verify gauge creation succeeds for arbitrary pair address
Quantify emissions redirectable to fraudulent gauge

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying fake contract + depositToken call (~0.05 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Gauge emissions per epoch directed to attacker's pair
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high if emissions are significant
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to create gauge; ongoing emission theft

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilutes legitimate gauge emissions; whitelists potentially malicious tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate gauge voters and LPs
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced emissions proportional to attacker's fraudulent gauge weight

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify fake pool can trigger full _preLaunchPool flow

threats.title: Abstain Votes Weaponized to Force Proposals into Expired State for Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorCountingSimple, _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes AND forVotes > abstainVotes. An attacker can cast abstain votes to ensure neither _voteSucceeded nor _voteDefeated returns true, pushing the proposal into Expired state. Combined with ECON-H001, the Expired proposal can still be executed via execute().
threats.investigation_rationale: The voting logic creates a third outcome (Expired) where neither success nor defeat is determined. Since execute() allows Expired state, an attacker can strategically use abstain votes to ensure their own proposal lands in Expired, guaranteeing execution regardless of community opposition.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes; _voteDefeated requires againstVotes > forVotes && againstVotes > abstainVotes

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient smNFT voting power to make abstainVotes > forVotes
Governor.sol variant (smNFT-based) is deployed and in use
Quorum is reached (forVotes + abstainVotes >= quorum)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _voteSucceeded: forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes; neither success nor defeat -> Expired -> executable
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit malicious proposal, cast large abstain votes so abstainVotes > forVotes, ensuring Expired state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Proposal executes despite majority opposition, bypassing governance

economic_questions: How much voting power is needed to force Expired state?
Can a single large smNFT holder force any proposal into Expired?
What is the cost of acquiring sufficient voting power?
validation_requirements: Verify state machine: quorum reached + neither succeeded nor defeated = Expired
Confirm Expired state is executable via execute()
Calculate minimum abstain votes needed given typical voter participation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to acquire smNFTs with sufficient voting power

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Quorum numerator, typical voter participation rates

threats.title: Single Broken Genesis Pool DoS Blocks All Epoch Flip Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: checkAtEpochFlip() and checkBeforeEpochFlip() iterate over all liveNativeTokens and make external calls for each pool. If any single pool's external call reverts (e.g., a malicious token that reverts on transfer, or a broken gauge), the entire transaction reverts, blocking all other pools from transitioning. The pre_epoch_period update in checkBeforeEpochFlip only occurs after all iterations, so it remains stale on revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: No try-catch wraps the per-pool operations. A single reverted call to pairFactory.setGenesisStatus, IGenesisPool.setPoolStatus, or addLiquidity blocks the entire batch. This creates a permanent DoS where the failing pool prevents all other pools from launching or transitioning.

threats.title: balanceOf Division by Zero When totalDeposits Becomes Zero After Launch
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.balanceOf() computes `(_depositerLiquidity * userDeposits[account]) / totalDeposits`. After launch, if deductAllAmount is called for all depositors (reducing totalDeposits to 0 via deductAmount/deductAllAmount), any subsequent balanceOf query for non-owner accounts divides by zero, reverting. This can break gauge integrations that query balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: deductAllAmount sets `userDeposits[account] = 0` but does not update totalDeposits. deductAmount does subtract from userDeposits but totalDeposits is never decremented in either function. However, after all depositors have deducted their full amounts, totalDeposits still reflects original value. The issue arises if userDeposits for all accounts reach 0 while totalDeposits remains positive, causing a logic inconsistency but not div-by-zero. But if totalDeposits were ever set to 0 through another path, div-by-zero occurs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balance = (_depositerLiquidity * userDeposits[account]) / totalDeposits with no zero-check on totalDeposits

preconditions: Pool has been launched (liquidity > 0)
totalDeposits could theoretically become 0 (edge case in accounting)
External contract queries balanceOf after accounting edge case

threats.title: Missing Deposit Window Enforcement Allows Risk-Free Sniping of Genesis Pools

threats.title: L2Governor propose() Passes block.number to Timestamp-Based Voting Token
threats.hypothesis_description: L2Governor.propose() calls getVotes(_msgSender(), block.number - 1) but _getVotes delegates to token.getPastVotes(account, blockTimestamp) where the second parameter is expected to be a timestamp. Passing block.number (~10^7) instead of block.timestamp (~10^9) queries votes at the wrong checkpoint, potentially returning 0. Currently mitigated by proposalThreshold() returning 0, but if threshold is ever set > 0, governance proposal creation becomes permanently bricked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The parameter naming 'blockTimestamp' and the use of timestamp-based deadline management throughout the Governor suggest timestamps are expected. Using block.number as a timestamp queries an epoch from 1970, which would return 0 votes for any modern account.

proposalThreshold() is overridden to return > 0 (currently returns 0, mitigating the issue)

threats.title: claimNative Lacks Reentrancy Guard Enabling Double Claim with Callback Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.claimNative() zeroes refundableNativeAmount before transfer but has no reentrancy guard. If the native token is an ERC777 or token with transfer callbacks, the callback could re-enter other claim functions or state-modifying functions. While direct re-entry to claimNative is safe (amount zeroed), cross-function reentrancy into claimIncentives or other state-changing paths during the callback could extract additional value.
threats.investigation_rationale: GenesisPool has no ReentrancyGuard. All claim functions (claimNative, claimDeposits, claimIncentives) make external token transfers. claimIncentives zeroes incentives per-token inside a loop - a callback during one transfer could re-enter to claim other tokens or call other functions.
deployment_assumptions: Native token or incentive token has transfer callbacks (ERC777, hooks)
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence specifying token types. nativeToken is user-provided via whitelist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimNative: zeroes refundableNativeAmount then calls safeTransfer with no reentrancy guard; claimIncentives (lines 219-229) zeroes incentives per-token inside loop before transfer

preconditions: Native token or incentive token implements transfer callbacks
Pool is in NOT_QUALIFIED or PARTIALLY_LAUNCHED status
Token owner is the caller (for claimNative) or any caller with deposits (for claimDeposits)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No ReentrancyGuard on GenesisPool; claimIncentives loop zeroes amounts one-by-one during iteration
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: During safeTransfer callback in claimIncentives loop iteration i, re-enter to manipulate state affecting iteration i+1
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Cross-function reentrancy could drain additional tokens or corrupt accounting

economic_questions: Can callback from one incentive token transfer enable re-entry that steals other incentive tokens?
What cross-function reentrancy paths exist from claimNative callback?
Are any whitelisted native tokens ERC777 or callback-enabled?
validation_requirements: Analyze claimIncentives loop for cross-iteration reentrancy
Test callback-enabled token in claimNative path
Check connector/whitelisted tokens for callback behavior

threats.title: FixedAuction.purchased() Is a No-Op Breaking Auction Purchase Tracking Interface
threats.hypothesis_description: FixedAuction.purchased() is called by GenesisPool.depositToken() after each deposit to notify the auction of purchased amounts, but the function body is empty. This means no purchase tracking occurs. While FixedAuction has a fixed price, custom auction implementations registered via AuctionFactory that rely on purchase callbacks for dynamic pricing would silently fail to update state, leading to incorrect pricing for subsequent depositors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The IAuction interface defines purchased(uint256 amount) as a state-changing callback. FixedAuction's implementation is an empty function with a '// populate' comment suggesting it's incomplete. Any dynamic auction type expecting this callback to track state would be broken.
deployment_assumptions: Future or alternative auction implementations rely on purchased() callback for pricing state
threats.config_evidence: FixedAuction.sol line 33: function body is empty with comment '// populate'; AuctionFactory allows adding custom auctions

proof_of_signal.pattern: function purchased(uint256 amount) external { } - empty body; called from GenesisPool.depositToken line 140

preconditions: A dynamic auction is registered via AuctionFactory
The dynamic auction relies on purchased() to update internal pricing state
Genesis pool is configured to use this auction

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: IAuction(auction).purchased(nativeAmount) called in depositToken but does nothing in FixedAuction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No access control on purchased() - any address can call it on custom auctions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Dynamic auction pricing manipulation if purchased() is externally callable without validation

economic_questions: Are any non-FixedAuction implementations deployed?
Does purchased() have access control in custom implementations?
Can external calls to purchased() manipulate auction pricing?
validation_requirements: Review all registered auction implementations
Test purchased() access control across auction types
Verify pricing consistency after manual purchased() calls

threats.title: Auction Address Can Be Set to Zero via Removed AuctionFactory Entries
threats.hypothesis_description: In GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken(), the auction is selected via auctionFactory.auctions(auctionIndex) with a fallback to auctions(0). If both the requested index and index 0 point to removed (zeroed) entries, the auction is set to address(0) in the genesis pool, causing all subsequent depositToken calls to revert when calling auction methods.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code: `auction = auctionFactory.auctions(auctionIndex); auction = auction == address(0) ? auctionFactory.auctions(0) : auction;`. If auctionFactory.removeAuction(0) was called, auctions[0] = address(0). Then both branches return address(0). setGenesisPoolInfo stores address(0) as the auction, and _getNativeTokenAmount will revert on any call to IAuction(address(0)).
deployment_assumptions: AuctionFactory owner removes the auction at index 0
threats.config_evidence: AuctionFactory.removeAuction() sets auctions[_pos] = address(0); no deployment evidence prevents removal of index 0

proof_of_signal.pattern: auction fallback to auctions(0) which can be address(0) if removed; no null check before setGenesisPoolInfo

preconditions: AuctionFactory owner removes the default auction at index 0
User calls depositNativeToken with an invalid or removed auctionIndex

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No null check on final auction address before use
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: address(0) stored as auction, bricking all deposit operations

analysis_questions: Is index 0 the only fallback? Could another fallback mechanism exist?
Can governance/admin recover by calling setAuction on the affected pool?
What's the blast radius: just one pool or all future pools?
validation_requirements: Remove auction at index 0, then call depositNativeToken
Verify genesis pool stores address(0) as auction
Verify subsequent depositToken calls revert

threats.title: Router update is functionally bricked; can force launch failures if router must be migrated
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.setRouter requires _router == address(0), making it impossible to set a valid router. If the deployed router becomes deprecated/compromised or needs changing, the protocol cannot update, causing persistent launch failures or forcing unsafe workarounds.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect that creates an operational dead-end; it may not be directly exploitable by an unprivileged attacker, but it materially increases outage risk during incidents.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setRouter(address _router) external onlyOwner { require(_router == address(0), "ZA"); router = _router; }

preconditions: A router migration is required (bugfix, upgrade, external dependency change) OR router is accidentally set to address(0)
GenesisPool.launch depends on GenesisPoolManager.router value
No alternative path exists to pass a correct router at launch time (manager uses stored router)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Owner can only set router to zero, which would brick all launch paths that call addLiquidity.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner action or incident scenario.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During incident response / planned router changes.

operational_questions: Is the router address immutable elsewhere or can genesis pools be launched with a router parameter bypassing manager storage?
Is there an upgrade path for GenesisPoolManager to patch this without disrupting state?
Has setRouter ever been called on-chain and with what values?
validation_requirements: Unit test: setRouter(valid) reverts; setRouter(0) succeeds; subsequent launch attempts revert
Assess upgrade/patch procedures (proxy admin, upgrade executor) in production
Determine whether epoch automation can be paused safely if router must change

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for router to require change or be deprecated.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If router breaks, launches fail indefinitely; if router is set to 0 (by mistake), launches brick immediately.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Operationally exploit stuck launches to harm protocol credibility (economic/market impact).
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires misoperation or emergency scenario; not purely on-chain by attacker.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Genesis launches can halt; cascading failures in epoch workflows that depend on successful launches.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium to high depending on upgrade governance and proxy control.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Liquidity launch flow for all genesis pools.

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low (but depends on needing router change).
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Triggered during router migration or incident.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setRouter calls; router being address(0); repeated launch reverts at router call site.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Epoch flip transactions failing consistently due to addLiquidity call failure.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Emergency upgrades or migrations attempted and failing.

threats.title: reserveNFTs() can exceed MAX_SUPPLY due to reservedAmount increment even when mints are capped by totalSupply() check
threats.hypothesis_description: reserveNFTs enforces reservedAmount + _amount <= MAX_RESERVE, but inside the loop it only mints if totalSupply() < MAX_SUPPLY. If totalSupply is near MAX_SUPPLY, fewer than _amount NFTs are minted, yet reservedAmount is still incremented by _amount. This can desync reservedAmount from actual reserved mints and potentially block future legitimate reservations or mislead accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete correctness bug in state accounting. While onlyOwner-gated (low realism under threat model), it can occur during normal admin operations and break invariants around reserve tracking.

proof_of_signal.lines: reserveNFTs(): conditional _safeMint inside loop but reservedAmount += _amount unconditionally
proof_of_signal.pattern: State counter increment not tied to actual mint count

preconditions: Owner calls reserveNFTs when totalSupply() is close to MAX_SUPPLY
totalSupply() < MAX_SUPPLY is false for some iterations, skipping mints
reservedAmount is relied upon for enforcement/analytics

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Reserved counter updated even if mint skipped
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ReservedAmount can exceed actual reserved mints, potentially blocking further reserves or distorting reporting
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No local mintedCount used for reservedAmount increment

analysis_questions: Is reservedAmount used elsewhere for enforcing max supply/reserves beyond this function?
Do admin procedures assume reservedAmount matches actual reserved NFTs?
validation_requirements: Test: set totalSupply near MAX_SUPPLY, call reserveNFTs with _amount > remaining supply, confirm reservedAmount increases by _amount while only remaining supply is minted

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner attempts to reserve 50 NFTs when only 10 supply remains; only 10 are minted but reservedAmount increases by 50, exhausting MAX_RESERVE accounting and preventing future reserves.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited; impacts sale operations and accounting rather than direct theft
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: NFT project operations; potential buyers if reserve logic affects sale planning
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires owner/admin correction via upgrade or manual compensation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge-case tests for reserves near MAX_SUPPLY

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Mismatch between reservedAmount and actual reserved mints

threats.title: Auction address can be set to non-auction contract, enabling malicious conversion math to misallocate deposits (economic manipulation)
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.setAuction (onlyManagerOrProtocol) accepts any non-zero address, with no verification against AuctionFactory.isAuction(). A malicious auction can return manipulated getNativeTokenAmount/getFundingTokenAmount causing deposits to be capped incorrectly, allocatedNativeAmount to be wrong, or eligibility thresholds to be met spuriously, impacting the launch ratio and depositor fairness.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even though access is privileged, it is a concrete code gap: the pool trusts auction math for critical accounting and threshold logic. Misconfiguration or malicious setting can lead to user harm.
deployment_assumptions: Manager or tokenOwner (per onlyManagerOrProtocol) sets auction to a contract not vetted by AuctionFactory
Users deposit based on expected pricing/ratio derived from auction
threats.config_evidence: No code-level enforcement that auction is registered in AuctionFactory; GenesisPool.setAuction only checks _auction != address(0).

proof_of_signal.lines: setAuction(): require(_auction != address(0)) and status check only; no factory/isAuction validation
proof_of_signal.pattern: Unvalidated external dependency for core accounting

preconditions: Caller is genesisManager or genesisInfo.tokenOwner
poolStatus == NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED (as required by setAuction)
Deposits/eligibility logic depends on auction conversion outputs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External oracle/math contract address not validated
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Malicious auction can skew allocations/threshold checks to favor token owner or deny depositors fair ratios

analysis_questions: Is AuctionFactory intended as an allowlist? If yes, why isn't it enforced in GenesisPool?
Can an attacker profit by setting auction to over-credit nativeAmount to reach eligibility early and then launch with bad ratios?
Are UIs/users relying on FixedAuction-style predictable math?
validation_requirements: Construct a malicious auction that returns extreme ratios; simulate depositToken allocations and eligibility transitions
Check whether GenesisPoolManager.setAuction (Governance) uses allowlisting or can be abused similarly

threats.title: Thenian reserveNFTs Increments reservedAmount Even When Minting Skipped at MAX_SUPPLY
threats.hypothesis_description: reserveNFTs increments reservedAmount by _amount AFTER the loop, regardless of how many NFTs were actually minted (the loop skips minting when totalSupply() >= MAX_SUPPLY). This means reservedAmount can reach MAX_RESERVE even if most reserve slots were wasted, permanently consuming the reserve allocation without corresponding NFT mints.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop has `if (totalSupply() < MAX_SUPPLY)` check inside, but reservedAmount is incremented unconditionally outside the loop. If the owner calls reserveNFTs(addr, 150) when totalSupply is near MAX_SUPPLY, only a few NFTs mint but all 150 reserve slots are consumed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reservedAmount = reservedAmount.add(_amount) is outside the for loop; loop body conditionally skips _safeMint but _amount still fully counted

preconditions: Owner calls reserveNFTs when totalSupply is close to MAX_SUPPLY
Some mint iterations are skipped

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: reservedAmount += _amount (always) vs actual mints (conditional per iteration)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Reserve allocation consumed without corresponding NFT mints
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Fewer reserved NFTs than MAX_RESERVE intended; accounting error

economic_questions: How many NFTs can actually be reserved in edge cases?
Does this affect the overall NFT supply distribution?
validation_requirements: Test reserveNFTs with totalSupply near MAX_SUPPLY
Verify reservedAmount vs actual minted count

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: reservedAmount vs actual mints at boundary

threats.title: Router update is bricked: setRouter only allows zero address, enabling permanent launch DoS if called (or preventing urgent router migration)
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.setRouter(address) contains an inverted require that only permits setting router to address(0). If executed (accidentally or maliciously via privileged owner), subsequent launch() calls will revert (router is zero). Even if not executed, the bug prevents legitimate router upgrades/migration, creating operational fragility.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect affecting critical operations (genesis launches). While it requires owner action, it is a realistic ops failure mode during incident response or upgrade attempts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setRouter (address _router) external onlyOwner { require(_router == address(0), "ZA"); router = _router; }

preconditions: Caller is GenesisPoolManager owner()
setRouter is called with address(0) (only passing value)
Genesis pools rely on GenesisPoolManager.router during _launchPool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Setter intended to update router cannot set a valid router

operational_questions: Who holds the owner() role for GenesisPoolManager in production?
Is router already immutable elsewhere or is this the only update path?
Are there emergency procedures that would attempt to rotate router during incidents?
validation_requirements: Confirm deployed GenesisPoolManager bytecode contains the inverted require
Simulate launch with router==0 to confirm full lifecycle DoS
Review operational runbooks/scripts for router changes
